name: Mugwort
description: Mugwort (Artemisia vulgaris) is a common perennial plant deeply entwined with European folklore and magical practices. Revered for its protective and visionary properties, it was believed to ward off evil spirits, disease, and misfortune. Its association with the moon goddess Artemis (Diana in Roman mythology) lends it a feminine and intuitive energy. Historically, mugwort was used in rituals for dreaming, divination, and astral travel. Hung over doorways or carried as an amulet, it provided a shield against negative influences. It was also employed in smudging rituals to cleanse spaces and objects of stagnant energy. Midsummer celebrations frequently featured mugwort, burned in bonfires to purify the atmosphere and ensure good fortune. In some regions, it was placed beneath pillows to induce prophetic dreams. Its use in love spells and as a charm for fertility further solidified its place in the cultural landscape. Beyond its magical applications, mugwort served as a medicinal herb, used to treat a variety of ailments, including digestive issues, menstrual problems, and nervous disorders. The enduring legacy of mugwort reflects its perceived power to connect with the unseen world and offer protection in both the physical and spiritual realms. Its connection to liminal spaces, such as doorways and thresholds, highlights its role as a guardian and facilitator of transitions.
subtitle: The protector and visionary herb of Europe.

keyMyths.title: Dream Incubation
keyMyths.description: Placing mugwort under your pillow is said to induce vivid and prophetic dreams, allowing for communication with the spiritual realm. This practice was used for guidance and problem-solving.

keyMyths.title: Protection from Evil
keyMyths.description: Hanging mugwort over doorways or carrying it as an amulet was believed to ward off evil spirits, disease, and negative energy. It served as a protective barrier against harm.
